sentences.text: Well, at that time already, they started to liquidate the ghetto.
It means they were coming at night with lists, certain people, to-- to take them out on transport.
We didn't know where they were going.
But the SS, with the Jewish Police together, they were making the list up.
And it was-- you couldn't get out.
You couldn't get out in the street to-- you know, you were trying to get something, get bread or some food or something.
You were trying to get out of the ghetto, to buy something from smugglers or get out and buy-- buy some food, something, to sell for jewelry or something, whatever you had.
Time were getting very tough.
And it was hard to get food.
We all had to register, register to get the-- the ration book.
And it was a official document which was called the [INAUDIBLE].
It was a German document with your photo on it.
The round outs.
All the round outs, all the roundups at night.
We didn't sleep at night.
And in the daytime, we had to go to work.
I worked on-- at the brick factory.
And going out to Bonarka, I worked, that time, on the loading up-- loading up-- loading bricks.
And you worked with a spade, with a spade cutting out blocks of lime from the fields, and putting it in-- into a wagon, which was on the rails.
And this was very hard work, just to lift up a block that you cut out-- cut out with a spade.
It was like very soft, like clay.
But it was very heavy work, very heavy work.
And the Germans were there.
The guards were there.
There were also guards, Ukrainian.
There were Lithuanian guards.
They were-- they were all joined, volunteers to the SS.
They were all assigned to the SS.
And they were worse.
Sometimes they wanted to show off to the Germans that they could be more cruel than the German themselves.
So--
The Jewish Police was just inside the ghetto.
They didn't have-- they couldn't go outside.
But we were under guard constantly, going out of the ghetto to work, to Bonarka.
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then I had a friend of mine who-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --tried to get me a job inside, to get inside the workshop.
The workshop.
And-- and I managed to get out of the to get into the workshop.
It was a good change for me.
Because I got into the blacksmith's shop.
And I-- I was learning to-- to help the blacksmith.
Anvil.
On the anvil, putting it on the anvil with a big pair of pliers.
And he had a little hammer, a little hammer.
And he was pointing with that little hammer where he wants you to hit.
There were three guys with big sledge-- big sledgehammer.
And was like, one, two, three, was hitting.
One, two, three, one, two, three, like an orchestra hitting it-- hitting it.
And we were forging that thing.
And God forbid if you didn't hit it right on the-- in the center of the anvil, and-- and his arm went up, he would throw this right into your face.
He could kill you.
Anyhow, I managed to work.
I was good.
I was hitting, hitting, hitting, together with the-- with the-- with the-- with the other two.
It was good job for me because I was in contact with Polacks, with Polish people, already, that I could-- I could exchange something with them.
If I could smuggle out some-- some clothing from there, and-- and I could exchange for it potato or something, I was able to bake a potato in the-- in the-- in the hearth already.
I could manage to get something.
Anyhow, I worked there for-- for-- for a little while.
Then this job came to an end.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I got another job.
Then the war broke out with the Russians.
Then make the ghetto smaller.
That were-- there was a lot of grabbing people at night.
And then with the dogs and shooting, and-- and-- and this was constantly going out, just to keep us.
Killing was a daily routine, the killing.
And then I got another job when the war broke out with Russia.
This was [INAUDIBLE].
This was the other side of the river, Vistula.
And we were going down there at night, in the evening, through the night.
We worked at night.
They were bringing-- this was, already I think, 1942, in winter.
I think so.
They were bringing German from the Russian front, frozen.
Frozen.
They were bringing in, at night, with the Red Cross, with Red Cross trains.
And they were-- they had typhus.
They has lice, the big of my thumb and this was [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what's [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] is to delouse them, to do the steam-- the steam and the showers and the baths, and everything.
And this was-- this was on a side of a railroad tracks.
And we had to carry them from the trains that they brought in from the Russian front.
They were moaning and groaning down there.
There were injured and so forth.
But most of them were young and frozen.
And we used to carry them.
There was no platform.
We used to carry them from one-- from-- form one railroad track.
Here was, on the right-hand side, was-- was the railroad track.
And we had to carry them and step down to the ground, then on the-- on the left side was another train, where [NON-ENGLISH] was.
And you had to carry them up the-- up the stairs.
There wasn't a even platform where-- where they-- but taking them down and then carried them on our backs, or when it was a guy was heavy.
So two of us were carrying him, and carrying him up, up, up, up, up the stairs.
And they had a lot of gold and Russian rubles.
And they had watches that they stole, and all kinds of things.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, we could eat something.
We could grab a piece of bread.
They-- they-- they wouldn't let us.
But there was plenty of food down there.
We could eat down there.
But we couldn't take nothing back home, back to the ghetto.
And-- and on one of those-- one in particular guy was a young SS man, blond and blue eyes.
And he was moaning.
Nothing was wrong with him.
His feet were frozen.
And he was telling me a story in the little broken Polish.
So I knew that he was from the border town of Poland, [INAUDIBLE], somewhere.
And he was saying to me that he was on the front.
And those Russians can stay for days and nothing happened.
He was just a few hours and his legs got frozen.
And he called me a friend.
I was so mad.
He was a big guy, maybe six feet.
And I grabbed him on my back and dragged him down the stairs and hit with-- with us.
I-- I was hitting-- hitting-- hitting with his feet, which were frozen, hitting the-- the stairs as I was coming down the-- the stairs and grabbed him.
But you couldn't hear him screaming.
Because everybody was moaning and screaming.
And then when I took him over, already, to delousing, when I dropped him off, I started to cry.
I started to cry myself.
I said dammit, I'm as bad as him already.
I-- I felt so bad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I hurt him.
Because I deliberately hurt him.
Because he called me a friend.
And-- and-- and he wasn't my friend.
He was my enemy.
October 1942.
Well, one of those-- one of those morning I was coming from the job that I was working at night with-- with-- with-- with-- with the Red Cross, working for the Germans.
I was coming down.
I heard, from a distance already, we were coming on the guard.
We were-- we go.
And before we left that place, [NON-ENGLISH], they were checking us, searching us.
Anybody had something, a watch or anything, right away, they shot them on the spot, right away.
Anyhow, coming to the ghetto, I saw a lot of-- a lot of police, unusual, outside the ghetto.
And as we're coming in, I heard the shots and everything, coming in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were a lot of people on this street, blood, a lot, all over.
And I ran into the-- we were living, [NON-ENGLISH] 5.
This was the-- a corner building.
I go into the house.
I don't see anybody.
I don't see-- my mother and my sister at that time worked for-- in-- in the Chocolate Factory, [NON-ENGLISH], which they converted into a manufacturing of German uniforms.
They worked as-- as-- as-- as a dress-- as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They were sewing.
Sewing the uniforms.
My sister and my mother.
And I came into the house.
And I don't see anybody.
I see a mess.
In the backyard and everything, I see a lot of-- a lot of bodies all over the place.
And I start looking for my mother.
And I don't see anybody nowhere.
And I go out.
I go out on [NON-ENGLISH], on [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were marching a lot of people towards [INAUDIBLE].
[CRYING] And as-- I see-- I see marching on the sidewalk.
And I go.
I look, I look, I look.
I saw-- I saw my mother.
And I see Miriam, my sister.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I see little Miriam petrified, hanging onto my mother's skirt, holding on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was the last time that I saw Miriam and my mother.
[CRYING] And I don't know where they took them.
After the war, I found out where they went.
I think [INAUDIBLE], or [INAUDIBLE].
I am not sure.
[CRYING]
My father and my two brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But my father wasn't already the man he-- that he was.
He didn't care anymore, anymore than we did.
We kids understood.
He already stopped shaving.
He didn't shave already.
He looked terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father, he wasn't, already, the tough guy who he was, already.
He was broken down physically, mentally.
He hardly spoke.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they started to liquidate the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Till they liquidated the ghetto.
They liquidated the ghetto.
I saw [INAUDIBLE].
Across the street from the ritual bath there was a little hospital.
There were little babies down there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were throwing out those babies through the windows.
And grabbing them, grabbing them by the legs and hitting their head against the-- the curb.
Right.
Maybe a little later.
I don't remember exactly.
Because the dates are very hard to remember.
But there are things that you never forget.
But dates, I don't-- I'm not good with dates.
But I know details about the rest of the story.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, on one of those-- one of those-- I just worked all those-- they were going from house to house and looking for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cleaning up all the-- picking up all the bodies, what they-- loading in up on the wagons and taking them down to Plaszow.
And of those outings, the last liquidation was-- it was already in the evening.
They took us down there.
There were graves digged out there.

And as we were throwing in those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people to the graves, to the big mass graves, they started to shoot at us, the few who-- who were left, who were doing the job, to finish the job.
And I just was just lucky.
I just threw myself in-- into the graves, with the dead bodies.
I was just covered with blood.
Covered-- covered with blood.
And then they came down-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --on the heap of the people.
There were thousands.
Some-- some were just wounded.
So they were just shooting with the gun in-- into the head who was moving or breathing, or whatever.
[SIGHS] Then at night I managed to lift myself up, and get up.
And I went to some-- some barracks.
And some people helped me, to wash up the blood.
And they gave me new clothing.
And in the morning they covered up the-- the-- with the earth, with-- the-- the whole-- the whole graves.
With those there-- with the dead-- with the dead ones.
Well, I wasn't long in Plaszow.
I wasn't long in Plaszow.
Father worked in the [?
Shumaker's, ?] [?
Shumaker ?] factory.
And Bernard worked with him.
And Sam was working for a little while in the-- in the fur, fur industry down there.
And I got a job going out to Bonarka again, to the brick factory.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then not long afterwards, we-- my brother and I somehow got together to Wieliczka Wieliczka [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a salt mine.
It was a salt mine.
And the German wanted to move all the industry, all the heavy industry under the-- underground into the-- into the salt mines.
So there won't be any-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] any threats of-- of-- of-- of-- of being bombed from the air, bombed.
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was already in 19-- 1944, beginning.
At night, we heard already-- we heard already, artillery.
At night, we could hear artillery.
And there was one, two-- I think four of us, four of us fellows.
We make the pact that we-- in case the Russians are approaching very close.
Or [GERMAN].
They were yelling and getting us to the elevator shafts and bringing us up to the-- to the top.
And the four of us make a pact that we're going t hide ourself in case the Russians are approaching or something like that.
And my brother and I were working somewhere near the shaft.
And the Germans came right away and got us out.
We didn't manage to hide ourself.
And the other two fellows managed to hide out, hide themselves.
It was Spielman and Fuchs, and Zenec Fuchs.
There were two guys.
And the-- the minute they brought us up, they started counting heads.
And there were two guys missing.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they went down with the dogs, with sniffing dogs.
And they found them.
Anyhow, the following day, they load us up on trucks.
And we were going, we don't know where.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I was manage-- I was sitting in the back on the trucks.
And there was a young SS man.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what he said to me.
Anyhow, we got back to Plaszow, the following-- the following day.
There was a hanging.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They hanged Zenec Fuchs and the Spielman.
They hanged them.
I don't remember who else they hanged.
But I remember we had to stand and watch it.
This wasn't the only hanging.
There were many hangings while I was in Plaszow, that we had to stay on the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] means the parade grounds, the big place that we always had.
They were constantly hanging and shooting, in Plaszow.
There was-- every day they were bringing in people and boom, boom, boom, boom, boom.
You-- you heard.
And they were burning.
They were burning, putting gasoline and blocks of wood and burning people.
You could smell.
Anyhow, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, from there--
Yes.
I saw-- I saw Bernard, and I saw my father, yes.
Yes.
Then we were loaded, I don't know, a few days later or what.
Oh, there was many incidents.
The-- the-- the guy in charge of the Plaszow was a guy by the name of [?
Getz. ?] He was a big fellow, a handsome fellow, maybe 6'2', maybe weighed 220, maybe.
Big fellow.
He was riding, always, on a white horse.
And he had a couple of dogs hanging-- having around him.
And he was like, going hunting for ducks.
He used to shoot people just-- just-- just like this, just for-- like a-- like hunting for ducks, like they do in this country.
He was very, very sadistic.
Very sadistic.
Once-- one incident he was-- we were working in Plaszow, up on the road, cleaning, cleaning something, a road, or moving mountains from one side the mountain.
The next day, the mountain was on the other side.
Just doing-- thousands of people were doing with shovels, with picks, with-- with-- with-- just moving one mountain to the other side, just to keep us working.
And one of his dogs bit my brother, Sam.
Grabbed him here, grabbed him here by the ribs.
He still has, today, the-- the marks from it.
And he was just lucky because nobody survived him.
A few more minutes.
OK, so-- and this was-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I don't remember, I know it was hot and summer.
Must have been the end of June, the beginning of August, when they started bringing in the trains assembling the cattle trains, for us to ship us out to different destination.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
OK.
